question_text: A 'strategic partnership' (as distinct from a vendor relationship) typically requires which of the following from both parties?

options.a: Long-term exclusive contract commitments on both sides involved.
options.b: Volume-based discount terms negotiated for the relationship.
options.c: Aligned interests, joint investment, and shared risk-reward.
options.d: Equity exchange between the two parties involved in the deal.

question_text: When a customer is in the middle of being acquired, the most appropriate commercial posture is generally:

options.a: Withdraw from the relationship until the acquisition completes.
options.b: Pause major commitments and engage carefully with both sides.
options.c: Continue selling as if nothing in the relationship has changed.
options.d: Push to close major deals before the acquisition completes.

question_text: An 'ecosystem strategy' (vs a product strategy) typically means:

options.a: Selling more products from the existing portfolio of offerings.
options.b: Diversifying the business into adjacent industries very quickly.
options.c: Building value through partners and complementor relationships.
options.d: Acquiring multiple companies in the same product category space.

question_text: When considering a strategic pricing change at company scale, the MOST important analysis dimension is generally:

options.a: Price elasticity and willingness-to-pay across segments.
options.b: Competitor pricing across the relevant market segments today.
options.c: Cost basis of the product across the entire production line.
options.d: Margin targets set by finance for the product line.

question_text: Direct sales versus channel (reseller / partner) sales models typically have which trade-off?

options.a: Direct and channel produce essentially identical outcomes.
options.b: Direct is always more profitable than partner channels.
options.c: Direct gives higher margin; channel gives faster reach.
options.d: Channel is always faster to scale than a direct salesforce.

question_text: From a CEO/board perspective, customer concentration above ~20% of revenue from a single customer is generally:

options.a: Optimal for operational efficiency in the relationship.
options.b: Not a concern provided the customer remains satisfied.
options.c: A material risk that affects valuation and financing terms.
options.d: A sign of strong execution and customer relationship strength.

question_text: A 'strategic account' (as opposed to a large account) is best defined by:

options.a: Account profitability measured against blended targets.
options.b: Account size measured by the revenue contributed annually.
options.c: Account longevity measured by years of active relationship.
options.d: Strategic importance beyond direct revenue contribution.

question_text: The 'bowtie' GTM model (replacing the funnel in modern SaaS thinking) represents:

options.a: Equal-weighted acquisition and expansion stages mirrored.
options.b: A more decorated version of the traditional sales funnel.
options.c: A pricing strategy used in modern SaaS company go-to-market.
options.d: An organisational structure for the modern commercial team.

question_text: In high-stakes negotiations, sending a negotiator with limited authority (the 'limited authority' tactic) can be effective because:

options.a: It is required by most major negotiation frameworks in use today.
options.b: It allows time to consult and prevents emotional escalation.
options.c: It is unethical and should be avoided in any serious deals.
options.d: It always speeds up the pace of complex negotiations greatly.

question_text: Systematic win/loss analysis at executive level should focus most on:

options.a: Pricing analysis on the deals won and lost in the quarter.
options.b: Individual sales rep performance compared with their peers.
options.c: Patterns across deals to inform strategy and positioning.
options.d: Sales activity volume across the team and the period.

question_text: You are the CEO. Your largest customer's CEO requests a one-on-one meeting. The MOST effective preparation is:

options.a: Diligence: their strategy, recent earnings, prepare 2-3 substantive ideas you might bring.
options.b: Prepare a comprehensive update on what your company is currently doing for them right now.
options.c: Have your sales team brief you on the current state of the customer relationship today.
options.d: Bring your senior sales lead with you to the meeting so they can field operational detail.

question_text: Your largest customer (15% of revenue) is signalling they may not renew. The MOST appropriate executive response is:

options.a: Begin scenario planning for the customer loss while continuing routine account engagement.
options.b: Engage exec sponsor; listen, take ownership, design substantive response; diversify also.
options.c: Delegate the recovery effort to your Chief Customer Officer to handle on your behalf.
options.d: Authorise whatever discount or commercial change is needed to retain the major customer.

question_text: You are negotiating a strategic partnership with a peer-company CEO. They are pushing for terms that favour them substantially. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Escalate the negotiation to your board for guidance on how to handle the unfair terms.
options.b: Hold firm on your position and prepare to walk away from the partnership entirely now.
options.c: Concede the disputed terms to get the strategic partnership signed and moving forward.
options.d: Pause commercial detail; re-engage at strategic level: define success for both 3+ years.

question_text: Your team has brought you a large deal to approve. The signals (customer behaviour during negotiation, post-sale service implications, strategic fit) suggest this will be a costly customer. The MOST disciplined response is:

options.a: Structured fit review: 5-year P&L with service cost, churn risk, opportunity; decide cleanly.
options.b: Decline the deal — strategic fit is paramount and this one is misaligned for the company.
options.c: Approve the deal — large revenue volumes are hard to turn down at this point in the quarter.
options.d: Approve the deal but raise the price significantly to compensate for the projected risks.

question_text: Your team proposes raising prices 15% on new customers. Demand is strong, switching costs are high. The MOST rigorous executive decision-process is:

options.a: Phase the increase: start with 5% across new customers first, then evaluate impact in 6 months.
options.b: Approve the price increase based on the strong team confidence in the proposal as it stands.
options.c: Defer the decision on price increases entirely — pricing changes are too risky to take on now.
options.d: Require rigour: elasticity, willingness-to-pay, competitor, signal; test on segment 90 days.

question_text: Your largest competitor has just been acquired by a much larger company with deeper pockets. Your team is alarmed. The MOST effective leadership response is:

options.a: Strategic review: capability change, where they'll move, defensible position, double down where.
options.b: Match the competitor's likely investment increase to maintain your relative market position.
options.c: Reassure the team that nothing has fundamentally changed in your competitive picture today.
options.d: Begin negotiating an exit or sale of your own company while the market is still strong now.

question_text: Your top 5 customers represent 60% of revenue. The MOST appropriate strategic response is:

options.a: Maintain top accounts while explicitly investing in adjacent segments over 18-36 months ahead.
options.b: Begin exploring acquisitions to diversify the customer base quickly through inorganic growth.
options.c: Continue current execution since the top 5 customers are profitable for the business today.
options.d: Reduce focus on the top 5 customers to allow diversification of the base to happen naturally.

question_text: Your sales channel partners are increasingly upset about your direct sales motion competing with them. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Continue with both motions and let channel partners compete with direct sales for the deals.
options.b: Design clear rules of engagement: which segments are which; hold consistently; lose short-term.
options.c: Eliminate the direct sales motion entirely to preserve the channel partnerships fully going forward.
options.d: Increase partner margins to compensate them for the direct competition they are facing today.

question_text: Your board is pushing aggressively for revenue growth that you believe would require unsustainable customer-acquisition spending. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Push back on the board's request and risk losing their confidence in your leadership of the company.
options.b: Begin looking for a new CEO role since the board's pressure has become unsustainable for you.
options.c: Execute the growth plan as the board has requested to maintain their confidence in your strategy.
options.d: Engage: present unit economics, CAC, LTV scenarios; explicit strategic choice; then defer.

question_text: You have decided to raise prices 12% on contract renewals. The MOST effective communication approach to existing customers is:

options.a: Email all customers simultaneously with the price increase announcement for transparency now.
options.b: Apply the price increase quietly at renewal without prior notification to the customers.
options.c: Tier approach: personal exec to strategic, structured to mid-tier, standard to long-tail; runway.
options.d: Have account managers communicate the price change during routine business reviews each.

question_text: Your team has been pursuing a transformative account for 18 months without conversion. The MOST disciplined executive decision-process is:

options.a: Decline future engagement with the account based on sunk cost from the past 18 months invested.
options.b: Continue pursuing the account — strategic pursuits of this size simply take time to convert.
options.c: Structured review: learnings, probability, opportunity cost; commit explicitly or withdraw cleanly.
options.d: Replace the lead pursuit team with a fresh team that may have new ideas for the account.

question_text: Two weeks before quarter-end, you are 8% short of guidance. The MOST mature response as CEO is:

options.a: Diagnose: quarter timing or structural? Sober call; transparent with board; don't engineer it.
options.b: Convene daily war-room sessions with the team to drive deal closure in the final two weeks.
options.c: Pull demand from the next quarter via aggressive incentives to make the current numbers work.
options.d: Push the team to apply aggressive discounting on open deals to close the gap before quarter end.

question_text: You are about to visit the CEO of an important customer. Your sales team has shared a detailed brief. The MOST effective use of pre-visit time is:

options.a: Memorise the operational details from the team's brief so you can demonstrate command of the account.
options.b: Prepare a proposal for additional business that you can pitch directly during the meeting today.
options.c: Read their recent earnings; understand worries; prepare one substantive question or perspective.
options.d: Prepare a comprehensive update of your company's roadmap to share during the customer meeting.

question_text: A potential customer is large and prestigious but their values and behaviour are incompatible with your company culture. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Negotiate aggressive contractual terms that protect against the projected cultural mismatch.
options.b: Decline transparently to team: "Here's why; here's what we protect. This is what discipline looks like."
options.c: Pursue the deal — the prestige value of having the customer's logo will compensate for the friction.
options.d: Decline the deal quietly without sharing any explanation with the customer or the wider team.

question_text: Two of your strategic partners are in dispute, and each is pulling you to take sides. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Side with the partner that is more important to your business strategically right now.
options.b: Encourage the partners to resolve the dispute before you continue engaging with either of them.
options.c: Engage neutrally: honest chats with each; decline sides; clear on principles; preserve both.
options.d: Stay out of the dispute between the two partners entirely without engaging on either side.

question_text: I am comfortable engaging at CEO/board level with substantive strategic conversation, not just relationship management.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can hold strategic patience on long-cycle pursuits without losing focus or pressure-discounting.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I hold commercial discipline at company scale, even when individual deals tempt away from it.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I think about my strategic accounts as a portfolio with deliberate investment patterns, not individually.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I model the discipline of walking away from bad-fit revenue for my team.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I think about my business as part of an ecosystem of partners, customers, competitors — not in isolation.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I have built recognised industry stature through publications, speaking, or visible thought-leadership.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: Under quarter-end pressure, I have approved discounts or terms that I later regretted.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I sometimes accept customers I should decline because of revenue pressure or board expectations.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: How often do you (personally, as a senior executive) spend time with your top customers' senior leadership? PILLAR 02  ·  ORGANISATIONAL LEADERSHIP AT SCALE Strategy and execution; capital allocation; board governance; culture; talent and succession; M&A; crisis leadership; and the integrated capabilities that distinguish exceptional senior leaders from competent ones. Heaviest weight - this is the central work at senior levels. Items in this pillar: 70

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: Roger Martin's 'Playing to Win' framework defines strategy as the answer to five integrated questions, the first of which is:

options.a: What management systems do we need to support the strategy?
options.b: What capabilities must we have to execute the strategy?
options.c: How will we win in the chosen markets and segments?
options.d: What is our winning aspiration for the organisation?

question_text: Galbraith's 'Star Model' of organisation design identifies five interlocking design points:

options.a: Strategy, Tactics, Goals, Roles, Metrics tracking.
options.b: Strategy, Structure, Processes, Rewards, People.
options.c: Plan, Organise, Lead, Control, Continuously Innovate.
options.d: Vision, Mission, Values, Culture, Capabilities.

question_text: In Will Thorndike's 'The Outsiders' analysis of exceptional CEOs, the single most important CEO skill identified is:

options.a: Capital allocation across investment alternatives.
options.b: Cultural leadership across the senior executive team.
options.c: Operational excellence across day-to-day execution.
options.d: Deep industry expertise built up over many years.

question_text: Research on M&A outcomes consistently shows that most acquisitions:

options.a: Fail to create value for the acquirer's shareholders.
options.b: Create more value than equivalent organic investment.
options.c: Create significant value for the acquirer's shareholders.
options.d: Have a neutral effect on shareholder value over time.

question_text: In corporate governance, the primary fiduciary duty of board directors is to:

options.a: Whichever stakeholder group is currently most vocal.
options.b: The CEO who appointed them to the board seat.
options.c: Quarterly earnings performance and market sentiment.
options.d: The corporation and its long-term shareholders.

question_text: Jim Collins's principle of 'first who, then what' from 'Good to Great' argues that:

options.a: Get the right people on board before deciding the direction.
options.b: Hire fast and fire fast to keep team performance high overall.
options.c: Hire only people who already agree with the chosen strategy.
options.d: People matter more than strategy in early-stage startup contexts.

question_text: Edgar Schein's framework for understanding organisational culture identifies three levels, from most visible to deepest:

options.a: Artifacts, Espoused Beliefs, Underlying Assumptions.
options.b: Visible Symbols, Memorable Slogans, Recurring Stories.
options.c: Observable Behaviours, Stated Values, Shared Vision.
options.d: Documented Policies, Skilled People, Repeated Processes.

question_text: Clayton Christensen's theory of 'disruptive innovation' specifically refers to:

options.a: Any innovation that significantly disrupts a market.
options.b: Technology breakthroughs that reshape an industry.
options.c: Any aggressive new entrant into an established market.
options.d: Cheaper offerings for overlooked segments, moving up.

question_text: Daniel Kahneman's work on decision-making distinguishes 'System 1' and 'System 2' thinking. Senior executives are particularly vulnerable to System 1 errors when:

options.a: They are tired, pressured, or trust their expert intuition.
options.b: Decisions are routine and have been made many times before.
options.c: They use spreadsheets to analyse the available options.
options.d: They consult with senior peers before deciding the issue.

question_text: In crisis communications research, the most effective initial leadership response is generally to:

options.a: Wait for full information before any communication is made.
options.b: Issue a brief denial until the facts are fully confirmed.
options.c: Communicate known and unknown promptly with accountability.
options.d: Defer all communication to the company's legal team only.

question_text: Research on CEO succession indicates that the single most predictive factor of successful transitions is:

options.a: Hiring an external successor with fresh outside perspectives.
options.b: Generous compensation packages for the incoming CEO leader.
options.c: A long deliberate process with multiple internal candidates.
options.d: Speed of transition to minimise the leadership uncertainty.

question_text: Edward Freeman's 'Stakeholder Theory' argues that businesses should be managed for:

options.a: The full set of interdependent stakeholders of the firm.
options.b: Government regulators as the primary external stakeholder.
options.c: Shareholders alone, with others considered only instrumentally.
options.d: Each stakeholder weighted equally across all decisions.

question_text: Research on large-scale organisational transformations consistently shows that the typical failure rate of major transformation initiatives is approximately:

options.a: 60–70% fail to deliver intended outcomes.
options.b: Failure rates are not reliably measurable.
options.c: 30–40% fail to deliver intended outcomes.
options.d: 10–20% fail to deliver intended outcomes.

question_text: 'Loss aversion' (Kahneman & Tversky) refers to the empirical finding that:

options.a: People discount future losses heavily in their daily decisions.
options.b: People avoid risks in general across most decision contexts.
options.c: People prefer certainty to uncertainty across all decisions.
options.d: Pain of losing is about twice the pleasure of equivalent gain.

question_text: Research on executive compensation design has consistently found that the strongest predictor of long-term alignment between executive and shareholder interests is:

options.a: Performance bonuses tied directly to quarterly financial targets.
options.b: Higher total cash compensation paid annually to executives.
options.c: Broad profit-sharing arrangements across the executive team.
options.d: Significant equity with long vesting and holding requirements.

question_text: As CEO, your board is pressuring you to hit short-term numbers that would require deferring critical strategic investments. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Defer the strategic investments quietly to hit the short-term numbers without further discussion.
options.b: Engage board with analysis: cost of hit vs deferral, 3-5 year value; make case with conviction.
options.c: Threaten to resign over the issue if the board insists on hitting short-term numbers this way.
options.d: Comply with the board's pressure — their position carries significant weight in the company.

question_text: A board director repeatedly pushes a position you believe is strategically wrong. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Structured 1:1 outside boardroom: understand reasoning; share yours; see if synthesis possible.
options.b: Lobby other directors privately against the position to build opposition in the boardroom.
options.c: Defer to the director's seniority and accept the position even though you believe it is wrong.
options.d: Publicly disagree in the board meetings to expose the bad position to all other directors.

question_text: An executive on your team is consistently underperforming. They were hired by you 18 months ago at significant cost. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Move them sideways to a less critical role where their underperformance won't impact as much.
options.b: Continue support — hiring decisions at this level often take more time to play out fully.
options.c: Direct conversation about the gap; clear improvement path with milestones; act if not delivered.
options.d: Hope it improves with more time and patience as they continue settling into the executive role.

question_text: Your CFO and CRO are in serious conflict over commercial pricing strategy. The MOST effective intervention as CEO is:

options.a: Structured session with both: present cases; surface underlying interests; decide openly; align.
options.b: Let the CFO and CRO work it out between themselves without your further involvement at all.
options.c: Split the difference between their two positions to be fair to both executives equally now.
options.d: Pick the position that aligns most with your own view on the commercial pricing strategy now.

question_text: Market signals strongly suggest the core business is in long-term decline. A pivot would be transformational and risky. The MOST mature CEO posture is:

options.a: Transparent with board and team; rigorous analysis; sequential decisions; carry the team through.
options.b: Delegate the strategic decision to a transformation office that can focus on the work full time.
options.c: Continue optimising the core business — its cash flow funds the future strategy of the company.
options.d: Announce a dramatic pivot immediately to capture market attention and signal strategic intent.

question_text: You've just been appointed CEO. You discover a deeply rooted culture of executive politicking and information-hoarding. The MOST effective intervention is:

options.a: Diagnose deeply; redesign reward systems; model the new behaviour; address politicians directly.
options.b: Bring in a top consulting firm to lead the culture transformation across the organisation soon.
options.c: Replace the executive team to break the old patterns of political behaviour across the company.
options.d: Announce a culture change initiative to signal that the new CEO takes culture seriously now.

question_text: Strategic necessity requires reducing headcount by 15%. The MOST humane and professionally responsible approach is:

options.a: Announce the reductions broadly first to manage the shock, then handle individuals afterwards.
options.b: Plan deeply: strategic criteria, severance exceeds standards, prepare managers, individual first.
options.c: Delegate the execution entirely to HR with broad criteria for them to apply across the company.
options.d: Use voluntary attrition naturally over a slower period to avoid the trauma of formal layoffs.

question_text: A board director with strong industry credentials is bringing pet-project ideas to every meeting that distract from priorities. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Tolerate the pattern — all boards have idiosyncratic directors with their own pet projects on hand.
options.b: Address it with the chair privately: "Could we structure agendas to channel expertise productively?"
options.c: Push the chair to remove the director from the board entirely at the next opportunity to do so.
options.d: Address the director directly in the next board meeting to flag the impact on the discussion.

question_text: A serious crisis hits - data breach, fatal accident, financial misstatement, or public scandal. The MOST effective initial response in the first 24 hours is:

options.a: Issue a brief initial denial of the crisis pending fuller facts becoming available later this week.
options.b: Wait for the full investigation to be complete before making any public statement on the crisis.
options.c: Mobilise rapidly: cross-functional team, communicate honestly, visible accountability, own it.
options.d: Have the PR team handle all communications during the first 24 hours of the unfolding crisis.

question_text: Your strongest business unit is generating substantial cash. Multiple stakeholders advocate different uses: dividends, buybacks, M&A, organic R&D investment, debt paydown. The MOST disciplined approach is:

options.a: Distribute the capital proportionally across all of the proposed options to keep stakeholders happy.
options.b: Defer the capital allocation decision to the CFO to determine the best use of the cash flow.
options.c: Apply rigorous opportunity-cost analysis: after-tax return, strategic optionality; non-obvious calls.
options.d: Follow the industry peer practices on capital allocation since they have already worked through it.

question_text: Your top team is highly competent but homogeneous (same background, similar perspectives). The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Invest in cognitive diversity: hire against gaps; red teams; pre-mortems; acknowledge as risk.
options.b: Rotate the team's exposure to different parts of the business to broaden their perspectives over time.
options.c: Accept the senior team since it is currently highly performing and getting results for the company.
options.d: Hire external advisors to bring different perspectives into the senior team's decision-making.

question_text: Your highest-performing executive consistently behaves in ways that violate stated company values (e.g., disrespect to peers, gaming systems). The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Tolerate the behaviour pattern since the executive's performance is critical to the company today.
options.b: Direct private chat: name the gap, values non-negotiable, agreed change with timeline; remove if not.
options.c: Address the issue through training or coaching to help the executive align with the values.
options.d: Reduce the executive's visibility across the organisation to limit damage from the values violations.

question_text: An activist investor has acquired a meaningful stake and is publicly criticising strategy. The MOST mature CEO response is:

options.a: Capitulate publicly to the activist's demands to avoid further public conflict on the strategy.
options.b: Engage with the activist exclusively through your company's lawyers as the official channel.
options.c: Refuse to engage with the activist publicly to maintain authority over the strategic narrative.
options.d: Engage openly: meet, listen to thesis, find legitimate critique, defend positions with evidence.

question_text: A critical executive (whose departure would significantly damage the business) has just told you they're considering an external offer. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Deep conversation about what they want next: address what's real; lose gracefully if right for them.
options.b: Make a heartfelt appeal to their loyalty and their commitment to the company over the years.
options.c: Accept the resignation gracefully to avoid being held hostage by retention conversations with them.
options.d: Counter with a significantly higher financial package immediately to retain them at the company.

question_text: A major social-political issue is dividing your customers and employees. Pressure mounts for the company to take a public stance. The MOST mature CEO response is:

options.a: Take the most popular position based on polling among your customers and your employees today.
options.b: Take a strong public stance that aligns with the dominant employee sentiment on the social issue.
options.c: Stay silent on the issue entirely to avoid alienating any of the customer or employee stakeholders.
options.d: Deliberate frame: relevant to business, stakeholders, or values? Clear principled stance; consistent.

question_text: Two of your board directors have a serious personal conflict that is affecting governance. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Have a private conversation with each director separately to understand their perspectives first.
options.b: Tolerate the conflict as a board matter that doesn't really require CEO direct intervention.
options.c: Side with the director whose position aligns more closely with your own on the governance matters.
options.d: Discuss openly with chair: "I want to flag what I'm observing. How would you like to address it?"

question_text: You're a long-tenured CEO and sense your own thinking has become stale. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Bring in challenging perspectives; question own assumptions; consider succession; genuine work.
options.b: Push harder on the existing company strategies that you have been executing as long-tenured CEO.
options.c: Continue in the role as is — stability of long-tenured leadership is valuable for the company.
options.d: Take an extended sabbatical from the role to refresh your thinking before returning to lead again.

question_text: An attractive M&A target has emerged. Your investment banker is enthusiastic. Strategic logic is plausible. The MOST disciplined approach is:

options.a: Hire McKinsey to validate the strategic fit of the M&A target with your company's strategy now.
options.b: Negotiate the price down significantly with the target to make the deal financially attractive.
options.c: Move quickly to lock in the M&A deal before competitors can bid up the target's price further.
options.d: Rigorous discipline: strategic fit, realistic synergy, integration, walk-away price, cultural fit.

question_text: Your compensation committee is proposing a significant increase in your pay package. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Engage: "Let's discuss design — cash vs vesting equity, how it compares to median employee pay."
options.b: Accept the increase but commit publicly to donating a portion of it to philanthropic causes.
options.c: Decline the proposed increase entirely to demonstrate selflessness to the board and employees alike.
options.d: Accept the proposed increase gracefully without raising any concerns about the structure of the package.

question_text: A major strategic initiative you championed has failed publicly. The MOST effective leadership response is:

options.a: Move past the failure quickly to avoid dwelling on it for too long with the leadership team.
options.b: Distance yourself from the decision that led to the failure to protect your overall credibility.
options.c: Own publicly: "I made the call. Here's what I missed. Steady, accountable, learning."
options.d: Identify the external factors that contributed to the failure to share with the leadership team.

question_text: You're building a new senior leadership team. The MOST important principle is:

options.a: Hire from your previous successful teams to ensure deep familiarity and high trust from day one.
options.b: Hire the most credentialed senior people available across the market for each of the open roles.
options.c: Hire for capability + values alignment + psychological maturity + complementarity with the team.
options.d: Hire for raw capability primarily across the senior leadership team being built for the company.

question_text: You've just taken over as CEO of an underperforming company. Stakeholders expect rapid action. The MOST disciplined first 90 days are:

options.a: Deep structured listening: 1:1s, observation, customer talks, board, financial; diagnose causes.
options.b: Replace the entire leadership team immediately to signal that significant change is coming now.
options.c: Wait the full 12 months in role before making any major changes to the company's strategy.
options.d: Announce a bold turnaround strategy in your first month to signal strong leadership to stakeholders.

question_text: Your business is mature and cash-generative but lacks innovation pipeline. Pressure exists to maintain margins. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Explicit choices: % cash for innovation; separation from core; accept failures; protect portfolio.
options.b: Maintain the existing margins — innovation is overrated for mature cash-generative businesses now.
options.c: Fund innovation reactively as opportunities arise rather than as a deliberate strategic priority.
options.d: Cut R&D dramatically and return the resulting capital to shareholders as a structural strategy.

question_text: Quarterly earnings are weaker than expected. Analyst pressure is high. The MOST effective communication is:

options.a: Emphasise the positive aspects of the quarter to manage the narrative for the sceptical analysts.
options.b: Commit to an aggressive next-quarter rebound to give the analysts a positive headline for the call.
options.c: Transparent: what happened, structural or temporary, what we're doing; acknowledge unknowns; defend.
options.d: Refuse to provide forward guidance for the next quarter to maintain maximum strategic flexibility.

question_text: You're a senior executive at a founder-led company. The founder makes decisions you believe are mistakes. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Defer to the founder's judgment on the company's strategic direction across all major decisions.
options.b: Implement the founder's decisions but quietly reduce your effort on the ones you disagree with.
options.c: Engage directly with respect: "Here's the reasoning. Help me understand your thinking on this."
options.d: Build a quiet coalition with other executives to influence change in the founder's decisions.

question_text: Your business has significant exposure to a country whose geopolitical situation is deteriorating. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Exit the country immediately to remove the geopolitical risk from the business's overall exposure.
options.b: Maintain operations in the country — markets often recover from geopolitical situations over time.
options.c: Hire a geopolitical advisor to monitor the situation and recommend actions to the executive team.
options.d: Scenario planning: develop concrete scenarios, decision triggers, optionality, indicators; sequential.

question_text: A respected senior executive is showing signs of plateau - same performance level for 3 years. The MOST effective coaching is:

options.a: Wait for them to retire naturally rather than addressing the plateau in their performance now.
options.b: Give them more responsibility to stretch their performance back upward over the coming year ahead.
options.c: Direct generous conversation: "Where's your next chapter? What would re-engage you?" Then co-design.
options.d: Replace them with someone hungrier for the next stage of growth in the executive's current role.

question_text: You need to inform the board of a serious operational miss that wasn't anticipated. The MOST mature delivery is:

options.a: Reach out to chair immediately; structured briefing: facts, root cause, response plan, your asks.
options.b: Wait until the next scheduled board meeting to share the operational miss with the full board.
options.c: Frame the issue positively to maintain the board's confidence in the executive team during the call.
options.d: Brief the directors individually via 1:1 calls or email before convening the full board together.

question_text: An investment opportunity has clear long-term value but takes 5+ years to pay off, with short-term P&L impact. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Make the investment quietly without explicit communication to avoid drawing scrutiny from analysts.
options.b: Pass on the long-term opportunity entirely to protect the company's quarterly performance numbers.
options.c: Make the case rigorously: NPV, rationale, downside; engage board; defend through near-term pressure.
options.d: Make a smaller hedged version of the investment to balance the long-term value with the near-term P&L.

question_text: You're considering taking on an executive coach. The MOST effective use of executive coaching at senior level is:

options.a: Use coaching sparingly — executive coaches are generally for people with significant problems to solve.
options.b: Deliberate use on themes: thinking partner, mirror for blind spots, work on specific patterns.
options.c: Have monthly catch-up sessions with the coach as a form of general professional support over time.
options.d: Use the executive coach as a strategy consultant on the company's most important business decisions.

PILLAR 03  ·  EXECUTIVE COMMUNICATION & INFLUENCE Board briefing, earnings calls, all-hands communication, crisis communication, written discipline, public presence, and the communication maturity expected at the top of organisations. Items in this pillar: 40

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: In executive briefings to boards, the most effective document structure is:

options.a: Bullet-pointed lists of all relevant facts in the case.
options.b: One-page executive summary with detail in the appendix.
options.c: Detailed analysis followed by the resulting recommendations.
options.d: Comprehensive narrative across background and the analysis.

question_text: Barbara Minto's Pyramid Principle structures arguments such that:

options.a: Each supporting point answers the question above it.
options.b: Arguments are presented in chronological order overall.
options.c: Most important points come last within the argument.
options.d: Each section is structured into exactly three sub-points.

question_text: In communicating organisational change to thousands of employees, the most effective approach is generally:

options.a: Clear narrative through multi-channel cascaded communication.
options.b: Individual conversations with each team across the organisation.
options.c: Town halls where leadership explains the new strategy in detail.
options.d: A detailed strategic memo distributed company-wide via email.

question_text: In Susan Scott's 'Fierce Conversations', the principle that 'the conversation is the relationship' means:

options.a: Difficult conversations should be avoided to preserve relations.
options.b: Conversations should focus on tasks and avoid feelings entirely.
options.c: All conversations are inherently relational by their nature.
options.d: Each significant conversation accumulates into the relationship.

question_text: Timothy Coombs' situational crisis communication theory recommends that for crises where the organisation has clear responsibility, the appropriate response is:

options.a: Express sympathy for those affected without admitting fault.
options.b: Full apology with concrete rebuild and remediation actions.
options.c: Deny responsibility while preparing the legal defence.
options.d: Issue a brief statement and allow the news cycle to pass.

question_text: In senior leadership listening, the most diagnostic form of listening is:

options.a: Listening primarily to formulate your reply.
options.b: Listening to confirm your existing views on the topic.
options.c: Listening for facts and information being presented.
options.d: Listening for what is unsaid in the conversation.

question_text: You're delivering a quarterly earnings call with mixed results. Analysts are sceptical. The MOST effective communication posture is:

options.a: Lead the call with the positive aspects of the quarter and downplay the misses to manage perception.
options.b: Provide conservative forward guidance for next quarter to reset expectations dramatically lower now.
options.c: Transparent: address misses directly; defend strategic positions with evidence; commit credibly.
options.d: Refuse to comment on the specifics of the quarter to maintain maximum strategic flexibility.

question_text: You're addressing the entire company (5,000+ employees) at a difficult moment. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Delegate the difficult parts of the all-hands to your Chief HR Officer to handle on your behalf.
options.b: Speak honestly: known and unknown; acknowledge impact; share conviction; open for genuine Q&A.
options.c: Deliver a fully prepared statement with no Q&A in order to maintain control over the communications.
options.d: Send a written memo to the entire company instead — less risk of saying the wrong thing live.

question_text: In a senior executive meeting, the CFO publicly disagrees with your strategic position in a way that feels personal. The MOST mature in-the-moment response is:

options.a: Stay silent in the meeting and address the public disagreement with the CFO privately later.
options.b: Match the CFO's energy in the moment with a sharp counter-point to defend the strategic position.
options.c: Stay composed: "I hear your concern. Let me address [point]." Address relationship privately later.
options.d: Defer to the CFO's view in the moment to maintain harmony with the senior team in the room.

question_text: A senior peer (same level as you) has been undermining your initiatives in conversations with your shared boss. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Mirror their undermining behaviour to even the playing field between you in the relationship.
options.b: Escalate the undermining behaviour to your shared boss directly to handle the situation officially.
options.c: Document the pattern of undermining and prepare to raise it formally with HR at a later point.
options.d: Direct: "I've heard reflections of you raising concerns. Want to find a better way to work together."

question_text: A major institutional investor has emailed concerns about your strategy. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Issue a public response to the investor concerns to address them more broadly with the market.
options.b: Reply personally: thank, address each concern with evidence, offer direct conversation if useful.
options.c: Have your investor relations team draft a polite, neutral response on your behalf to the investor.
options.d: Decline to respond to the email since investor relations should be the channel for such concerns.

question_text: You need to brief the board on a major issue. The MOST effective document is:

options.a: A short email summary of the major issue sent to the board ahead of the next meeting on it.
options.b: 1-2 page memo: issue, what happened and why, our response, asks from board, key risks; appendix.
options.c: A comprehensive 20-page analysis covering all of the angles of the major issue facing the board.
options.d: A verbal briefing only since written records of major issues can become discoverable in litigation.

question_text: You're being interviewed by a major business journalist who is known to be sceptical of your industry. The MOST effective preparation is:

options.a: Prepare scripted answers to all of the anticipated questions to control the interview narrative.
options.b: Speak entirely off-the-cuff during the interview to seem maximally authentic to the journalist.
options.c: Decline the interview entirely to avoid the risk of saying something that backfires later on.
options.d: Identify 2-3 key messages; prepare authentic substantive responses; practice bridging from hard.

question_text: You're CEO of a founder-led company. The founder regularly contacts your executives directly, bypassing you. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Direct conversation: "Direct contact creates confusion. Let's find a pattern that uses your insight."
options.b: Resign from the CEO role if the founder's bypassing behaviour continues without any change.
options.c: Tolerate the founder's behaviour — the founder has the right as the company's founder to do it.
options.d: Instruct your executives to redirect founder communications back to you as the company's CEO now.

question_text: Your company has clearly caused harm to customers. The MOST effective public apology is:

options.a: Full responsibility: name the wrong, the harm, apologise; describe remediation; follow through.
options.b: Have your legal team review every word of the apology for liability exposure before issuing it.
options.c: Express regret about the situation publicly without acknowledging the company's specific fault.
options.d: Apologise broadly while emphasising the mitigating factors that contributed to the harm caused.

question_text: Your largest customer is requesting a contractual concession that would set damaging precedent. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Agree to a private version of the concession without making any public contractual commitment.
options.b: Concede the contractual point to the largest customer to keep the major commercial relationship.
options.c: Direct: "Precedent across our customer base would damage us. Let's address what's driving this."
options.d: Refuse the request and let the relationship with the largest customer cool over the medium term.

question_text: In senior leadership team meetings, you notice quieter members rarely contribute on strategic topics. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Restructure the senior leadership team toward more vocal personalities to improve the discussions.
options.b: Call on the quieter team members by name during senior leadership meetings to draw their input out.
options.c: Accept the dynamic — those team members who want to speak up on strategic topics eventually will.
options.d: Redesign: pre-share questions; round-robin; written input; 1:1s with quiet; check your own behaviour.

question_text: A serious crisis (data breach, layoffs, regulatory action) requires immediate communication to all employees. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Write it personally with help: direct, honest, specific, own where appropriate; all-hands next day.
options.b: Send a brief statement to all employees immediately and follow up with details over the coming days.
options.c: Delegate the crisis email entirely to your CHRO and brand team to manage the communication.
options.d: Wait for the full information about the crisis to be available before any communication is sent out.

question_text: You're keynoting a major industry conference. The MOST effective preparation is:

options.a: Read the prepared remarks verbatim during the keynote to maintain maximum message control today.
options.b: Improvise the keynote based on the energy in the room at the time of the actual presentation.
options.c: Identify 1-2 take-aways; build around them with stories; rehearse to internalise flow, not words.
options.d: Rehearse the prepared keynote speech until it is perfectly memorised word for word for the event.

question_text: You're negotiating a major partnership with a senior leader from a culture with very different communication norms (high-context, hierarchical, relational). The MOST effective adaptation is:

options.a: Push for efficient transactional discussions to align with how the negotiation should proceed today.
options.b: Maintain your direct, content-focused style — they will adapt to professional norms over time.
options.c: Invest substantially in relationship first; adapt to their style; respect hierarchy; bring patience.
options.d: Bring a local cultural intermediary for all interactions to bridge the communication style gap.

question_text: Your 360 feedback indicates you are perceived as 'intimidating' and 'difficult to disagree with'. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Investigate who specifically said what in the 360 feedback to understand the underlying concerns.
options.b: Take seriously: reflect with coach; pick 2-3 behaviours to commit; share openly with team; track.
options.c: Defend yourself against the feedback — decisive leadership at senior levels requires clarity in style.
options.d: Soften your overall communication style across the board to address the 360 feedback received.

question_text: You're addressing employees during layoffs. Some are openly hostile. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Move quickly to the next steps and the Q&A portion of the all-hands to keep the agenda moving.
options.b: Match their energy in the moment to assert leadership authority during the difficult announcement.
options.c: Stay present and composed; acknowledge the legitimacy of their pain; honest; take hard questions.
options.d: End the all-hands session early to avoid further escalation of the hostility from the employees.

question_text: You need to communicate a complex strategic shift to senior leadership. The MOST effective format is:

options.a: Email summary of the strategic shift followed by individual conversations with each senior leader.
options.b: Amazon-style memo: structured prose, clear logic, depth; read silently; substantive dialogue follows.
options.c: Slide deck for visual impact during the senior leadership meeting about the strategic shift today.
options.d: Verbal briefing in the next senior leadership meeting to communicate the complex strategic shift now.

question_text: A long-term friend and colleague has stopped performing at the level the role requires. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Continue providing support out of loyalty to the long-term friendship between you over the years.
options.b: Move them to a different role at the company to avoid having the difficult performance conversation.
options.c: Have HR handle the difficult performance conversation with your long-term friend on your behalf.
options.d: Honest chat despite friendship: "The role evolved beyond what's working. Talk about what's right."

question_text: As a senior leader, you understand that symbolic acts (where you sit in meetings, who you spend time with, what you visibly prioritise) carry signal. In a moment of organisational stress, the MOST effective symbolic act is:

options.a: Hold a major leadership offsite to signal stability and continuity during the organisational stress.
options.b: Spend visible sustained time with front-line teams most affected: show up, listen, act on what.
options.c: Make a high-visibility executive change to signal that leadership takes the stress moment seriously.
options.d: Issue a strong public statement to acknowledge the organisational stress moment to all the staff.

question_text: You are preparing your annual letter to shareholders. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Highlight the year's successes; mention misses briefly to satisfy public disclosure requirements only.
options.b: Use the standard template the company has refined over years of writing shareholder letters.
options.c: Hire a professional writer to craft a polished, professional shareholder letter on your behalf.
options.d: Genuine reflective: own misses; explain logic; share what you're uncertain about; long-term partners.

question_text: How often do you personally engage front-line employees and customers (not through layers of leadership)? PILLAR 04  ·  SUSTAINED SENIOR PERFORMANCE Executive isolation, identity beyond role, sustained energy over decades, family integration, anti-power-corruption practices, and the wellness foundations that determine whether a senior career ends in renewal or burnout. Items in this pillar: 25

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: Research on executive isolation (the 'lonely at the top' phenomenon) consistently identifies that the strongest mitigations are:

options.a: Peer relationships outside the chain plus coaching support.
options.b: Holding more all-hands meetings across the wider organisation.
options.c: Reading more leadership books and listening to podcasts regularly.
options.d: Spending more time with family during personal off-time.

question_text: Research on the psychological effects of holding power consistently finds that:

options.a: Power tends to make people more healthily decisive.
options.b: Sustained power reduces empathy and feedback intake.
options.c: Power has no measurable psychological effect on leaders.
options.d: Power tends to make people more empathetic over time.

question_text: Research on post-role identity for senior executives (especially CEOs) suggests that those who navigate transitions best:

options.a: Took on multiple corporate board roles immediately afterwards.
options.b: Stayed engaged with their previous companies as advisors only.
options.c: Built identity beyond the role years before transitioning.
options.d: Continued in active executive roles as long as possible.

question_text: You have been in a senior role for years. Energy is declining. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Reduce your workload at the company secretly without raising it openly with the board or team.
options.b: Push through the energy decline with willpower until the natural cycle of the role shifts again.
options.c: Examine sleep, exercise, work; redesign week; take real break; be honest about role fit; decide.
options.d: Take vitamins and supplements to boost your energy levels during the demanding senior role period.

question_text: Your family is showing signs of strain from your role demands. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Tell the family this is the demand of senior roles and they need to adjust to it accordingly.
options.b: Promise to do better at home and try to manage the strain with more efficiency at the company.
options.c: Serious signal: deep talk at home; structural changes; sustainable rhythms; accept trade-offs.
options.d: Increase the household help to relieve the family's logistical load and reduce some of the strain.

question_text: You realise your identity has become almost entirely fused with your senior role. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Plan for early retirement from the senior role to refocus on who you are outside it eventually.
options.b: Take time off from the role to figure out who you are outside the senior role at the company.
options.c: Take seriously: invest in interests outside role as serious identity work; build over years not weeks.
options.d: Continue as is — identity-role fusion is generally normal and expected at senior levels of business.

question_text: You've been struggling with low mood and anxiety for months. The role makes it hard to admit. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Engage a mental health professional confidentially; this is medical care, not leadership weakness.
options.b: Push through the symptoms — showing any weakness at this level undermines your leadership authority.
options.c: Try more meditation and exercise to address the persistent low mood and anxiety on your own first.
options.d: Take a sabbatical from the role to figure out the underlying issues before returning fresh later.

question_text: The most effective post-intense-work-cycle recovery at senior level typically involves:

options.a: Complete digital disconnection from all work systems for the entire duration of the recovery period.
options.b: Working a lighter schedule for the next few weeks rather than taking a fuller recovery break now.
options.c: Two-week vacation to a relaxing destination with your phone on for the occasional important call.
options.d: Disciplined active recovery: real disconnect, sleep, activity, restorative people, different challenge.

question_text: You realise you're isolated at the top - few people you can speak honestly with. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Build structures: peer CEO group, serious coach, pre-role friendships, close internal relationships.
options.b: Accept the isolation at the top as one of the natural costs of taking on senior leadership roles.
options.c: Confide more in your direct reports about the things you previously kept fully to yourself.
options.d: Talk more with your spouse about the details of work to ease the isolation you feel at the top.

question_text: You realise you no longer love the role, but it remains prestigious and well-compensated. The MOST honest response is:

options.a: Continue in the senior role — love for the work is a luxury at this senior level of leadership today.
options.b: Explore what you want next; test small experiments; transition over 2-3 years with intentionality.
options.c: Quit the role dramatically and figure out the next chapter once you have created some space.
options.d: Reduce your engagement in the current role while continuing to collect the full compensation package.

question_text: Multiple crises hit simultaneously (business issue, family stress, personal health concern). The MOST effective leadership posture is:

options.a: Acknowledge to inner circle; protect recovery; consequential decisions when calm; lean on team.
options.b: Power through the simultaneous crises hitting to demonstrate resilience to the team and the board.
options.c: Maintain a stoic public face while privately struggling with the simultaneous crises hitting today.
options.d: Take immediate leave from the role to deal with all of the crises before returning to lead again.

question_text: How often do you spend genuine social time with peers outside your organisation? PILLAR 05  ·  TECHNOLOGY & DIGITAL STRATEGY GOVERNANCE Strategic engagement with AI, data, cyber, and digital transformation from the senior leader perspective: governance, capital allocation, talent, regulatory awareness, and the technology-strategy fluency expected of modern senior business leaders. Items in this pillar: 30

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: From a C-suite perspective, the most important strategic question about generative AI in 2025-2026 is:

options.a: Where AI changes the basic economics of our business.
options.b: How many AI engineers to hire over the next two years.
options.c: Which AI vendor to standardise on across the organisation.
options.d: Which business functions to automate first using AI.

question_text: An effective enterprise AI governance framework typically includes:

options.a: Purely technical security review of AI vendor deployments.
options.b: A blanket ban on AI use until vendors prove their safety.
options.c: Risk-tiered policies, guardrails, and human oversight design.
options.d: Letting each business unit set its own internal AI policies.

question_text: From a board oversight perspective, cybersecurity should be:

options.a: A regular board agenda item with quarterly reporting.
options.b: Reviewed annually as part of enterprise risk management.
options.c: Handled by the CISO without direct board involvement.
options.d: Delegated entirely to the audit committee oversight.

question_text: In modern strategic thinking, the primary competitive value of enterprise data comes from:

options.a: Regulatory compliance reporting across required jurisdictions.
options.b: Compounding strategic advantages through insight and AI.
options.c: Selling data to third parties in regulated marketplaces.
options.d: Cost reduction in internal reporting and analytics work.

question_text: The most common failure mode of large-scale digital transformation programs is:

options.a: Inadequate selection of the right enterprise technology choice.
options.b: Slow technology procurement processes across the firm.
options.c: Treating it as a technology project, not a business shift.
options.d: Insufficient budget allocation to the transformation programme.

question_text: From a senior strategic perspective, 'technical debt' is best understood as:

options.a: A purely engineering concern with no business implications.
options.b: Strategic constraint that limits future business agility.
options.c: A normal business cost with no strategic implications at all.
options.d: Something to be eliminated entirely across all systems.

question_text: For a senior executive, the most important emerging regulatory areas for digital business in 2025-2026 typically include:

options.a: Only ESG reporting standards across capital markets.
options.b: Only data privacy regulations such as GDPR and DPDP.
options.c: Only tax and trade regulation across the major jurisdictions.
options.d: Privacy, AI, cybersecurity, content, antitrust, data flows.

question_text: In senior decisions about whether to build or buy technology capability, the most important factor is:

options.a: Whether the capability is strategically core or commodity.
options.b: Vendor reputation in the relevant technology category.
options.c: Cost — the cheaper of build versus buy is usually right.
options.d: Speed — the faster of build versus buy is usually right.

question_text: From a governance perspective, the most important constraint on enterprise use of generative AI for consequential decisions is:

options.a: The speed of response from the production AI systems.
options.b: The risk of confidently false outputs (hallucinations).
options.c: The cost of API usage scaling with the volume of calls.
options.d: The available training data the AI model was built on.

question_text: In evaluating major technology vendors, the MOST important strategic risk consideration is:

options.a: Concentration risk and reversibility of the vendor choice.
options.b: Price competitiveness across the vendor and its market rivals.
options.c: The vendor's growth trajectory over the past few years.
options.d: Feature richness offered in the vendor's current product set.

question_text: Your CTO advocates a major bet on a specific AI strategy. The MOST mature CEO response is:

options.a: Defer entirely to the CTO's deep technical expertise on the proposed major bet for the AI strategy.
options.b: Make a small experimental commitment to the AI strategy now to maintain future strategic optionality.
options.c: Engage: how this changes business model, realistic vs hype, downside, reversible; you decide.
options.d: Wait for industry leaders to commit first to the AI strategy before making the major bet yourself.

question_text: Your company has just suffered a major data breach affecting millions of customers. The MOST effective initial CEO response is:

options.a: Issue a brief statement on the breach and let the legal team handle all of the rest of the work.
options.b: Wait for the investigation to fully complete before any external communication about the data breach.
options.c: Defer all the data breach communications to the CISO and the legal team to handle on your behalf.
options.d: Personally own: rapid communication, full mobilisation, transparent, remediation; visible accountability.

question_text: Your team proposes deploying AI-driven decision-making in a customer-impacting process. The MOST appropriate governance response is:

options.a: Approve the AI deployment if the technical team is confident in the model's overall accuracy.
options.b: Allow the AI deployment to proceed with monthly review meetings about its performance and impact.
options.c: Reject all customer-impacting AI use cases in customer-facing processes for the next 24 months.
options.d: Require assessment: risk class, thresholds, oversight, human-in-loop, audit; then decide carefully.

question_text: Your business is constrained by an ageing core technology platform. Modernisation is expensive and risky. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Approve a major replacement initiative based primarily on the CTO's recommended plan and approach.
options.b: Defer modernisation entirely to protect the company's short-term margins for the next few years.
options.c: Engage strategically: legacy cost over 5 years vs modernisation outcome; right approach; transparency.
options.d: Hire McKinsey to recommend the right modernisation path for the legacy platform across the company.

question_text: Your business has become deeply dependent on a single cloud / AI / SaaS vendor. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Continue with the current vendor — switching vendors at this point is too expensive to consider now.
options.b: Assess explicitly: failure scenarios; design reversibility; multi-vendor where feasible; manage risk.
options.c: Wait for problems to materialise with the vendor before acting on the deep dependency you have built.
options.d: Switch vendors immediately to reduce the deep dependency risk before the situation gets any worse.

question_text: Mid-crisis, your security team reports they cannot guarantee the breach is fully contained. Regulators require disclosure timeline. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Disclose minimally to meet just the legal requirements only without going beyond the strict letter.
options.b: Have outside counsel handle all the disclosure decisions to maintain legal privilege over the process.
options.c: Delay the regulatory disclosure to ensure the information about the breach is more fully complete.
options.d: Disclose within the required window honestly: known, investigating, response, what to do; prompt + honest.

question_text: Your CTO proposes a major foundational investment that will pay off in 5-7 years (data platform, model infrastructure, security overhaul). The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Approve only the portion of the investment that shows clear ROI within the next 18 months of activity.
options.b: Take seriously as consequential decision; bring board in; explain logic; defend through pressure.
options.c: Approve the proposed investment at half the proposed budget to share the risk with the technology team.
options.d: Defer the proposed investment — the payoff horizon is too long for committee approval to support it now.

question_text: AI capability is making 15% of your workforce roles redundant over 2-3 years. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Announce a hiring freeze and let natural attrition handle most of the workforce reduction over time.
options.b: Bring in management consultants to manage the workforce reduction process across the impacted teams.
options.c: Quietly reduce the affected headcount as the AI capability comes online over the next 2-3 years.
options.d: Transparent: communicate logic; invest in reskilling; demonstrate paths; severance for leavers.

question_text: Your company struggles to attract senior technology talent because the comp packages and engineering culture don't compete with tech-native peers. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Accept the reality of the current situation and continue to hire the best senior technology talent available.
options.b: Outsource technology work to specialist firms rather than building the in-house senior tech capability.
options.c: Acquire smaller tech-native companies specifically for the senior technology talent on their teams.
options.d: Address strategically: build competitive proposition or accept disadvantage; this is board-level conversation.

question_text: You use significant generative AI in customer-facing operations (chat, recommendations, content). The MOST appropriate disclosure approach is:

options.a: Hide the AI use from customers to maintain their perceived service quality from the company's brand.
options.b: Disclose the AI use only where regulation requires it in the relevant jurisdictions you operate in.
options.c: Transparent: indicate where AI vs humans, what it's used for, provide pathways to human assistance.
options.d: Don't disclose the AI use to customers — it has become standard industry practice across most companies.
